subtopics.title: Types of Functions
common_misconceptions: A function can have two outputs for one input
Inverse function means reciprocal
fog = gof (composition is not commutative)
subtopics.teaching_note: Function: rule that assigns each element in domain to exactly one element in range. One-one (injective): different inputs give different outputs. Onto (surjective): every element in range is mapped. Bijective: both one-one and onto (has inverse). Composite: fog(x) = f(g(x)), apply g first then f. Inverse: f⁻¹ swaps domain and range. Worked example: If f(x) = 2x+3 and g(x) = x², find fog(2). g(2) = 4, f(4) = 11.

subtopics.title: Matrix Operations
common_misconceptions: Matrix multiplication is commutative
det(A+B) = det(A) + det(B)
A matrix with determinant zero is the zero matrix
subtopics.teaching_note: Matrix multiplication: (m×n) × (n×p) = (m×p). Element (i,j) = row i of A · column j of B. det|a b| = ad-bc. Inverse A⁻¹ = (1/det) × adj(A). Cramer's rule: x = det(Aₓ)/det(A). |c d|

subtopics.title: Linear Inequalities and Optimization
common_misconceptions: The optimal solution is always at the center
Constraints don't need to be graphed
Inequalities are solved the same way as equations (sign flips when multiplying by negative)
subtopics.teaching_note: Steps: 1) Define variables. 2) Write constraints as inequalities. 3) Graph constraints to find feasible region. 4) Identify corner points. 5) Evaluate objective function at each corner. 6) Choose optimal. Worked example: Maximize P = 3x + 2y subject to x+y ≤ 10, x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0. Corner points: (0,0)=0, (10,0)=30, (0,10)=20. Maximum at (10,0).

subtopics.title: Rules of Differentiation
common_misconceptions: Product rule: d(uv)/dx = du/dx × dv/dx (NO, it's u·dv/dx + v·du/dx)
Chain rule is only for composite functions (that's exactly what it's for)
Second derivative zero means point of inflection (not always - need sign change)
subtopics.teaching_note: Product rule: d(uv)/dx = u(dv/dx) + v(du/dx). Quotient rule: d(u/v)/dx = (v·du/dx - u·dv/dx)/v². Chain rule: dy/dx = dy/du × du/dx. Stationary points: dy/dx = 0. Second derivative test: d²y/dx² > 0 → minimum, d²y/dx² < 0 → maximum. Worked example: Find max/min of y = x³ - 3x². dy/dx = 3x² - 6x = 0 → x = 0 or 2. d²y/dx² = 6x - 6. At x=0: -6 < 0 → max. At x=2: 6 > 0 → min.

subtopics.title: Techniques of Integration
common_misconceptions: Integration is just reverse differentiation (mostly true but techniques needed)
Definite integral always gives positive area (can be negative if below x-axis)
Trapezium rule gives exact area (it's an approximation)
subtopics.teaching_note: ∫xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C (n ≠ -1). ∫(ax+b)ⁿ dx = (ax+b)ⁿ⁺¹/[a(n+1)] + C. Area under curve: ∫ₐᵇ y dx. If curve goes below x-axis, split into positive and negative parts. Area between curves: ∫ₐᵇ (y_top - y_bottom) dx. Trapezium rule: ½h[(y₀+yₙ) + 2(y₁+y₂+...+yₙ₋₁)] where h = (b-a)/n.

subtopics.title: Circle Theorems and Applications
common_misconceptions: x² + y² = r² is the only circle equation (that's for center at origin)
Tangent is perpendicular to radius at point of contact
All lines that touch the circle are tangents (must touch at exactly one point)
subtopics.teaching_note: Centre-radius form: (x-a)² + (y-b)² = r² where (a,b) is center, r is radius. General form: x² + y² + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0, center = (-g, -f), radius = √(g²+f²-c). Tangent at (x₁,y₁): xx₁ + yy₁ + g(x+x₁) + f(y+y₁) + c = 0. Length of tangent from external point (x₁,y₁): L = √(x₁²+y₁²+2gx₁+2fy₁+c). Intersecting chords: PA × PB = PC × PD.

subtopics.title: Counting Principles
common_misconceptions: Permutation and combination are the same (permutation: order matters, combination: order doesn't)
nCr = nPr (NO, nCr = nPr/r!)
subtopics.teaching_note: nPr = n!/(n-r)! (arrangements where order matters). nCr = n!/[r!(n-r)!] (selections where order doesn't). nCr = nC(n-r). Worked example: How many ways to select 3 students from 10? ¹⁰C₃ = 10!/(3!×7!) = 120. How many arrangements of 3 from 10? ¹⁰P₃ = 10!/7! = 720. Circular permutation: (n-1)!. With repetition: nʳ.
